Option C is incorrect because 25G needles may help in selected technically difficult stations, but they are not superior as a first-line choice. Option D is incorrect because core-style needles may increase visible tissue fragments in some studies, yet current comparative data do not justify routine replacement of standard 21/22G TBNA needles in uncomplicated nodal staging cases.

questions.prompt: A 68-year-old former smoker has bulky 4R adenopathy and suspected metastatic lung adenocarcinoma. The bronchoscopy unit has both a standard 22G EBUS needle and a 22G fine-needle biopsy (FNB)/Franseen-type needle. The assistant suggests making FNB needle the default in this scenario because the patient will likely need next-generation sequencing. Which statement best reflects current evidence?

options.label: FNB may yield more visible tissue core, but overall diagnostic yield and safety are similar in current comparative studies
Molecular profiling is suboptimal when performed on samples obtained from standard 22G or smaller-sized needle EBUS-TBNA samples
FNB should be avoided because it causes more serious complications than standard 22G needles
FNB is suggested whenever molecular testing is anticipated

questions.explanation: Current data do not support making a 22G FNB or Franseen-type needle mandatory whenever molecular profiling is anticipated. In randomized comparative work, FNB needles have produced more visible tissue clot core, but overall diagnostic yield, adequacy, and complication rates were similar to those obtained with standard 22G EBUS needles. For lung cancer, the more important practical factors are adequate nodal targeting, sufficient passes, routine cell block preparation, and triaging material for ancillary studies. Recent guidelines specifically advise at least four passes when diagnostic testing and molecular profiling are needed and supports routine cell block preparation. Option B is incorrect because standard 22G EBUS-TBNA samples support immunohistochemistry and molecular testing when acquisition and processing are optimized. Option C is incorrect because current comparative data do not show a clear serious safety hazard that would justify avoiding FNB needles altogether. Option D is incorrect because FNB needles are not mandatory; they are reasonable tools in selected centers, but existing evidence supports standard 22G sampling as acceptable for biomarker-driven lung cancer evaluation.

questions.prompt: A 52-year-old woman presents with fevers, weight loss, night sweats and bulky subcarinal and right paratracheal lymphadenopathy. During cpEBUS-TBNA, the first three passes yield scant lymphocytes, and ROSE is nondiagnostic for a definitive process. An attempt at a second station yields similar results. An experienced operator is present and the node remains well visualized. Which next step is most appropriate?

options.label: Continue TBNA with additional passes of the same lymph nodes for further ROSE evaluation.
Add EBUS-guided intranodal miniforceps biopsy through the puncture tract.
Perform transbronchial lung biopsy.
Conclude the case and refer for surgical excision of an accessible suspicious lymph node.

questions.explanation: When lymphoma is suspected and TBNA cytology with ROSE is nondiagnostic, EBUS-guided intranodal miniforceps biopsy (EBUS-IFB) is one of the advanced cpEBUS techniques available to help provide more tissue architecture. Current guidelines recommend IFB only as an additional modality for experienced operators, particularly after negative ROSE or when a tissue biopsy is required for a definitive diagnosis, such as lymphoma. Meta-analytic data show that combining IFB with TBNA improves overall diagnostic yield and substantially increases lymphoma yield compared with TBNA alone. Option A is incorrect because simply continuing TBNA at a location where ROSE has been unyielding does not address the main limitation, which is inadequate tissue architecture. Option C is incorrect because transbronchial lung biopsy samples lung rather than the abnormal mediastinal node and is not the most direct next step in the absence of parenchymal lung disease. Option D is incorrect at this stage because bronchoscopic options have not been exhausted. However, if advanced techniques also prove unyielding, a surgical excision will be necessary.

questions.prompt: In which scenario is the incremental diagnostic benefit of mediastinal cryobiopsy best supported by current data?

options.label: A patient with suspected sarcoidosis or another benign mediastinal disorder after nondiagnostic TBNA.
First-line staging of a PET-positive malignant node that yielded diagnostic TBNA material.
Sampling of a densely calcified node to reduce the risk of needle breakage.
To complement cell block preparation for suspected lung cancer cases.

questions.explanation: The strongest incremental role for mediastinal cryobiopsy is in benign mediastinal disorders and certain uncommon tumors, such as lymphoma, especially when standard TBNA has been nondiagnostic or only scant material is obtained. In the randomized trial by Zhang and colleagues, cryobiopsy improved overall diagnostic yield versus TBNA alone and showed its clearest advantage in benign disorders, particularly sarcoidosis, while the yields for metastatic lymphadenopathy were already very similar between approaches. This is why cryobiopsy is best viewed as a targeted escalation technique rather than a universal addition to or replacement for TBNA. Option B is incorrect because routine first-line cryobiopsy for every malignant node that is already diagnostic on TBNA is not practical, cost-efficient, or supported by current data. Option C is incorrect because densely calcified nodes raise technical concerns, and cryobiopsy is not selected to solve that problem. Option D is incorrect because cell block preparation remains valuable in malignant disease; cryobiopsy does not add much, if any, utility to the standard cytology and cell block workflow in all cases.

questions.prompt: During EBUS-guided transvascular needle aspiration of a para-aortic node, ROSE on the first pass reveals malignant cells. The patient remains stable and the material appears adequate for cell block. What is the most appropriate next procedural strategy?

options.label: Continue with additional transvascular passes to gain additional tissue for molecular studies.
Convert to intranodal forceps or cryobiopsy through the vascular tract.
Use the existing material for cell block and ancillary studies.
Continue with additional transvascular passes to obtain samples for culture testing.

questions.explanation: The main procedural principle after a positive first-pass ROSE result during transvascular sampling is to minimize additional vascular traversals. Current guidelines suggest ROSE in all EBUS-TVNA cases to reduce the number of passes. Once malignant cells have been identified and the specimen appears adequate for cell block and ancillary studies, the safest approach is to stop or sharply limit further passes rather than continue sampling reflexively which increases risk. Option A is incorrect because multiple transvascular passes increase risk without clear benefit once diagnostic adequacy is achieved. Option B is incorrect because converting to forceps or cryobiopsy through a vascular tract would add unnecessary procedural risk and is not standard practice. Option D is incorrect because malignant cells have already been identified and culture is not needed.

assessments.title: Post-lecture 17-18 quiz: Complications and special scenarios

questions.prompt: A 61-year-old woman with suspected stage I sarcoidosis is scheduled for outpatient cpEBUS-TBNA. She is not taking antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy and has no liver disease, kidney disease, personal bleeding history, family bleeding history, or active bleeding. The bronchoscopy scheduler asks which labs to perform in preparation for the procedure. Which test(s) are most appropriate?

options.label: Proceed to cpEBUS without routine testing
Order PT/INR only
Order CBC count only
Order PT/INR and CBC

questions.explanation: Current cpEBUS guidance does not recommend routine preprocedure coagulation or platelet testing in patients without bleeding risk factors. In a low-risk outpatient with no anticoagulant use, no hepatic or renal disease, and no personal or family bleeding history, routine testing adds little value and may delay care without improving safety. Testing is generally reserved for patients at higher bleeding risk or those already receiving anticoagulants. The other options are less appropriate because selective PT/INR alone (option B) or platelet count alone (option C) is not recommended in an otherwise low-risk patient, while ordering both tests in all patients (option D) contradicts current guidance.
tags: Post-lecture 17-18 quiz: Complications and special scenarios

questions.prompt: A 58-year-old woman with a lung nodule and mediastinal adenopathy is scheduled for EBUS-TBNA to evaluate for suspected lung cancer. She is afebrile, immunocompetent, and has no prosthetic heart valve. Which prophylactic antibiotic strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin on morning of the procedure
IV cefazolin during the bronchoscopy
IV cefazoin during the bronchoscopy only if multiple nodes are sampled
Do not give prophylactic antibiotics

questions.explanation: Routine prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended before standard EBUS-TBNA. Infective complications such as mediastinitis, pneumonia, or abscess formation can occur, but they are very rare, and available prospective and retrospective data have not shown a clear benefit from routine antibiotic prophylaxis in unselected patients. Good procedural asepsis, sterile handling of the needle and stylet, and thoughtful target selection are more important preventive measures than empiric antibiotics. Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (or clindamycin in penicillin-allergic individuals) just prior to the procedure is the standard prophylactic regimen for dental procedures, and this regimen can also be considered in this setting for such cases. However, it is not needed routinely and, as with dental procedures, this approach should be reserved for high-risk patients, such as those with mechanical heart valves or compromised immunity (option A incorrect). A single-dose IV cefazolin during cpEBUS-TBNA can be considered in selected cases such as when an advanced technique is used that results in a large transmucosal portal for bacterial translocation into the mediastinum (e.g., forceps or cryobiopsy) or in the rare situation when a sterile space is accessed, such as aspiration of a bronchogenic cyst or vascular structure. However, it is not needed routinely (option A incorrect). The decision to give intraoperative antibiotics should not depend on the number of TBNA passes or nodes sampled, as there is no evidence to support this practice (option C incorrect).
tags: Post-lecture 17-18 quiz: Complications and special scenarios

questions.prompt: A 45-year-old man is referred for convex-probe endobronchial ultrasound (cpEBUS) after computed tomography shows a well-circumscribed 3.5 cm subcarinal cystic lesion without a solid component. On retrospective review, the cyst was present and similar in size and appearance on a CT performed one year prior at an outlying facility. The patient is asymptomatic and otherwise healthy. How should this lesion be approached?

options.label: EBUS-TBNA with a standard 21G/22G needle
EBUS-TBNA, but only with a 25G needle
Surgical excision
CT surveillance

questions.explanation: Routine EBUS-TBNA of a mediastinal bronchogenic cyst is generally discouraged because cystic mediastinal lesions carry a comparatively high risk of infectious complications, including cyst infection, mediastinitis, rupture, or pneumonia. Current guidelines specifically advise reserving diagnostic aspiration for situations in which alternative approaches are impractical or have already failed. This is primarily a patient-selection issue, not a needle-size issue, and there is no evidence favoring one needle type over another, either for safety or efficacy reasons (options A and B incorrect). Surgery is the definitive management for a bronchogenic cyst. However, this approach is reserved for cases that are symptomatic or for cysts that have an atypical appearance or behavior, such as having a part solid component or growing unexpectedly (option C incorrect). In such cases that are poor surgical candidates, cpEBUS-TBNA with antibiotic prophylaxis can be considered.
tags: Post-lecture 17-18 quiz: Complications and special scenarios

questions.prompt: A 69-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on apixaban is scheduled for EBUS-TBNA of a positron emission tomography-avid 4L node. She has no bleeding symptoms and her hemoglobin is stable. Which procedure preparation plan is most consistent with current guidance?

options.label: Continue apixaban routinely
Continue apixaban only if rapid on-site evaluation is available
Convert apixaban to enoxaparin before the procedure
Stop apixaban before EBUS-TBNA for the interval recommended for that specific anticoagulant

questions.explanation: Even though the overall bleeding rate of EBUS-TBNA is low, current guidance recommends interrupting anticoagulants before the procedure according to the interval advised for the specific drug. The low baseline bleeding risk of EBUS-TBNA does not make direct oral anticoagulants exempt from periprocedural planning. The correct approach is agent-specific interruption, with individualized thromboembolic assessment when needed. Options A and B are not appropriate as apixaban should be held regardless of ROSE availability. Switching from a direct oral anticoagulant to enoxaparin is typically not recommended practice due to the relatively short half-life of the former (option C incorrect). Instead, this approach is followed in patients on warfarin for high-risk conditions, such as mechanical heart valves, atrial fibrillation with high CHADS2 score, or recent venous thromboembolism.
tags: Post-lecture 17-18 quiz: Complications and special scenarios

questions.prompt: A 68-year-old man undergoes outpatient cpEBUS-TBNA under bronchoscopist-directed moderate sedation and without an airway adjunct. During sampling of the second node, his oxygen saturation transiently falls to 85%. No bleeding is seen and he remains hemodynamically stable. What is the likely explanation for the desaturation?

options.label: Pneumothorax
Pulmonary embolism
Pneumonia
Hypoventilation

questions.explanation: Transient hypoxia is the most common immediate complication reported during EBUS-TBNA. As with routine bronchoscopy, the etiology is likely multifactorial, including sedation- and transient upper airway obstruction-related hypoventilation and regional atelectasis. The short-lived desaturation usually improves with airway repositioning, implementation of naso-oral adjuncts, supplemental oxygen and, when needed, lightening or reversal of sedation. Pneumothorax and pulmonary embolism are exceedingly rare during cpEBUS-TBNA (option A and B incorrect). While respiratory infection may be an underreported complication in real-world practice, it would develop within a few days after, not during, the procedure (option C incorrect). cpEBUS complications MCQs cpEBUS complications MCQs
tags: Post-lecture 17-18 quiz: Complications and special scenarios

questions.prompt: A 69-year-old man has a known diagnosis of lung adenocarcinoma, but requires mediastinal staging. A cpEBUS sampling of up to 3 enlarged nodes is planned and rapid on-site evaluation is available. He has had no issues with previous sedation, does not have sleep apnea or cardiorespiratory comorbidity. Resources supporting all listed options are available. Which sedation plan is preferred for this case according to current guidelines?

options.label: Minimal sedation with liberal application of topical lidocaine.
Moderate sedation using midazolam and fentanyl.
Deep sedation/general anesthesia using propofol and remifentanil
Either moderate or deep sedation/general anesthesia is acceptable

questions.explanation: Both moderate sedation and deep sedation/general anesthesia are acceptable for CP-EBUS, as they provide similar diagnostic yield, especially for routine, fairly straightforward cases such as the one described in the stem (option D correct). Minimal sedation with topical anesthesia (option A) is not the preferred strategy for CP-EBUS (or any flexible bronchoscopy) due to inferior patient intolerance of the procedure compared to heavier sedation regimens. Moderate sedation (option B) may be preferred in uncomplicated cases, resource-constrained environments with limited access to anesthesia services, and for patients with certain comorbidities that may be exacerbated by general anesthesia. In contrast, deep sedation/anesthesia may be the better choice for a longer and more technically complex combined case, such as multistation nodal staging, need for sampling of a technically challenging peripheral lesion, ROSE unavailability, in patients with comorbidities that may be exacerbated by agitation, and trainee participation. These factors all may increase procedure duration and favor tighter control of airway, patient motion, hemodynamic status, and ventilation. However, most of these factors are absent in the case presented (option C incorrect).

questions.prompt: A 72-year-old woman with presumed non-small cell lung cancer has a 3.4 cm central right hilar mass. PET/CT shows a single, enlarged FDG-avid 4R node. During CP-EBUS, 4L, 7, and 2R nodes are all also visible and suitable for sampling. Rapid on-site evaluation is unavailable. Which sampling strategy best aligns with current guideline-directed staging?

options.label: Sample only the PET-avid 4R node
Perform a systematic mediastinal examination, starting with station 4L and then sampling the remaining accessible mediastinal stations that meet cpEBUS size criteria.
Sample the primary tumor first and conclude nodal staging once malignancy is confirmed.
Sample the station 4L node and then use sonographic appearance to select additional targets.

questions.explanation: Systematic CP-EBUS staging is preferred over targeted sampling of only suspicious nodes in presumed lung cancer. AABIP guidance emphasizes that a systematic examination should be performed: at least one node at every station greater than 5mm in size on short axis by EBUS exam. This detects additional occult mediastinal disease compared with targeting only abnormal stations on pre-procedure imaging. In practice, this means sampling in an orderly highest-stage-to-lowest-stage fashion, beginning with contralateral mediastinal disease before ipsilateral stations when feasible. Sampling only the PET-avid 4R node (option A) risks missing occult disease in normal-sized or PET-negative nodes. Sampling the primary tumor first and truncating nodal staging after confirming malignancy (option C) confuses diagnosis with comprehensive staging. Sonographic features alone (option D) may help prioritize nodes, but they do not eliminate the need for tissue confirmation.

questions.prompt: A patient with suspected lung cancer is scheduled for CP-EBUS under general anesthesia. Stations 4R, 7 and 11R are the likely sampling targets. He has severe diabetic gastroparesis and obesity. Which airway approach is the best choice for this cpEBUS case?

options.label: Transnasal insertion without an artificial airway.
Transoral insertion via supraglottic airway device.
Via endotracheal tube.
Via rigid bronchoscope.

questions.explanation: An endotracheal tube is the best choice here because aspiration risk is the dominant concern. Current guidance states that a supraglottic airway device is generally the preferred conduit when CP-EBUS is performed under general anesthesia, largely because it facilitates the procedure well and preserves access to higher mediastinal targets. However, that general preference does not override a clear need for a more secure airway in a patient at high risk for aspiration. A transnasal approach without an artificial airway (option A) would not be appropriate for this general-anesthesia, high-aspiration-risk case. A supraglottic airway (option B) is preferred for most routine general-anesthesia EBUS cases, but it is less protective against aspiration than an endotracheal tube. Furthermore, upper mediastinal nodes are unlikely targets in this case. A rigid bronchoscope provides no advantage for EBUS (option D incorrect).

questions.prompt: CP-EBUS-guided transbronchial needle aspiration of an enlarged 4R node is being performed in a patient with suspected lung adenocarcinoma, and molecular profiling is anticipated if malignancy is confirmed. Which sampling technique is most consistent with current guideline recommendations?

options.label: Use a 19-gauge needle or cryoprobe to enhance tissue acquisition for molecular analysis.
Use a 21-gauge or 22-gauge needle and perform at least 10 agitations per pass.
Use suction in favor over slow-pull or capillary sampling during aspiration.
Limit sampling to 2 passes when ROSE is available

questions.explanation: A standard 21-gauge or 22-gauge needle is appropriate for routine CP-EBUS-TBNA in suspected lung cancer. Current technical guidance supports at least 10 agitations per pass, and while 3 passes may be an acceptable diagnostic minimum in many settings, additional samples are recommended when molecular testing is anticipated. In practical guideline-based teaching, 4 or more passes per node is a reasonable target for diagnosis plus downstream biomarker analysis (option B correct, D incorrect). Routine use of larger-gauge needles, cryobiopsy, or intranodal forceps (option A) is not routinely recommended for lung-cancer evaluation; these are better reserved for selected low-yield situations or prior nondiagnostic sampling. Suction is not mandatory (option C), because both suction and no-suction/slow-pull approaches are acceptable. Limiting the node to only 2 passes (option D) risks inadequate tissue, especially for molecular profiling. The broader specimen-handling plan should also include preparation of direct smears and a cell block.

questions.prompt: A patient with suspected lung cancer is undergoing CP-EBUS-guided transbronchial needle aspiration at a center where a cytopathologist is not always available in the bronchoscopy suite. Which specimen-handling plan best aligns with current guidance?

options.label: Prepare direct smears and a cell block routinely
Use direct smears only; reserve cell block preparation for selected cases after a nondiagnostic result.
Defer CP-EBUS unless rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) is available for the first pass.
Replace smears and cell block preparation with liquid-based cytology alone.

questions.explanation: Current guidance supports performing CP-EBUS with or without rapid on-site evaluation. ROSE is often helpful when available, but its absence should not prevent the procedure from being done. At the same time, current specimen-handling guidance recommends that cell blocks be prepared routinely in addition to direct smears, because the combination supports diagnosis and downstream testing more reliably than smears alone. Using direct smears alone (option B) risks wasting valuable diagnostic and molecular material. Deferring the entire procedure until ROSE is available (option C) is not required by current guidelines and is not practical. Replacing both smears and cell block with liquid-based cytology alone (option D) is also not supported as the routine default. Some centers may use additional processing approaches, but CP-EBUS can proceed without ROSE, and routine creation of both smears and a cell block is a high-value specimen-handling standard.

questions.prompt: A 70-year-old man with a 3.5 cm central lung tumor and small hilar lymph node undergoes a technically complete systematic CP-EBUS mediastinal staging examination that is negative for malignancy. There are no large or intensely FDG-avid mediastinal nodes and no discordant imaging finding that was left unsampled. Which post-EBUS approach best matches recent guidance?

options.label: Proceed with confirmatory mediastinoscopy before definitive treatment.
Repeat CP-EBUS with a different operator.
Proceed to definitive management.
Repeat PET/CT

questions.explanation: Recent AABIP guidance does not support routinely adding mediastinoscopy after a negative, technically complete CP-EBUS mediastinal staging in patients who have risk factors for occult disease but no overt radiographic mediastinal involvement. The added diagnostic yield of routine confirmatory mediastinoscopy in this setting appears limited, whereas the extra procedure adds cost, inconvenience, and potential delay of care. Routine mediastinoscopy for all such patients (option A) therefore overstates the need for surgical confirmation. Mediastinoscopy remains important selectively: when there are large or intensely FDG-avid nodes that were negative on cpEBUS despite a discordant clinical picture, or when the initial CP-EBUS examination was incomplete. Repeating CP-EBUS (option B) is unnecessary when the first examination was complete and systematic. Repeating a PET/CT is unlikely to add new information and will delay management (option D).

questions.prompt: A 58-year-old woman is found to have a 1.8 cm pure ground-glass left upper lobe nodule with no solid component. CT and PET show a radiographically normal mediastinum and no suspicious hilar nodes. She is otherwise fit for surgical resection. Which staging plan best aligns with recent guidance?

options.label: Perform preoperative CP-EBUS-guided mediastinal staging.
CP-EBUS only if rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) is available.
Perform mediastinoscopy before surgery.
No routine invasive mediastinal staging.

questions.explanation: Routine invasive mediastinal staging is not recommended for a pure ground-glass nodule with a radiographically normal mediastinum. Recent AABIP guidance highlights that pure ground-glass nodules carry an extremely low risk of mediastinal lymph node metastasis. Therefore, the procedural burden and risk of routine CP-EBUS or mediastinoscopy are unjustified since a change in management is very unlikely in such patients (options A and C incorrect). The answer would be different for a part-solid lesion with a substantial solid component, a tumor larger than 3 cm, a central lesion, or abnormal N1/N2 findings on imaging. Making ROSE availability the deciding factor (option B) misses the key point: the issue is not specimen adequacy, but that invasive nodal staging itself is not routinely indicated in this particular phenotype.

questions.prompt: A 45-year-old woman with suspected stage I/II sarcoidosis has isolated station 4L, 5 and 7 lymphadenopathy. During a prior bronchoscopy, she had repeated agitation, continuous coughing, and intermittent desaturation despite adequate moderate sedation preventing completion of the procedure. Of the following options, which is the best next approach if moderate sedation must be used again?

options.label: EUS-B-FNA of station 4L and/or 7
cpEBUS with transvascular TBNA of station 5
cpEBUS using intranodal forceps or cryobiopsy of station 4L and/or 7
cpEBUS using a 19G needle of station 4L and/or 7

questions.explanation: For patients whose targets include stations 7 and 4L and have poor tolerance of airway bronchoscopy, EUS-B-FNA is well supported by randomized data as a reasonable alternative with similar diagnostic yield and better procedural tolerance. Studies comparing EUS-B-FNA with EBUS-TBNA for mediastinal adenopathy and sarcoidosis show broadly comparable yield for these stations, while EUS-B often causes less cough, shorter procedure times, and less oxygen desaturation. Option B is incorrect because transvascular sampling is not a comfort strategy; it is a specialized salvage approach for an otherwise unreachable lesion. Option C is incorrect because intranodal forceps or cryobiopsy are adjunctive tissue-acquisition techniques, and may be even more stimulating and poorly tolerated under moderate sedation than TBNA. Option D is incorrect for similar reasons.

questions.prompt: A 72-year-old man with a drug-eluting coronary stent placed 6 weeks ago has a positron emission tomography-avid subcarinal node that requires tissue confirmation. He is taking aspirin 81mg and clopidogrel 75mg, has no active bleeding, and has a normal platelet count. Which is the cpEBUS preparation plan most consistent with current EBUS guidance?

options.label: Stop aspirin for 7 days and continue clopidogrel
Continue aspirin; clopidogrel may be continued if thrombosis risk is judged to exceed bleeding risk after cardiology input
Stop both agents for 7 days
Bridge with low-molecular-weight heparin and stop both antiplatelet agents

questions.explanation: : Aspirin can be safely continued for EBUS-TBNA, and clopidogrel may be continued when the patient's thrombotic risk is judged to exceed the bleeding risk, especially in patients with recent coronary stenting (option B correct). The overall bleeding risk of EBUS-TBNA is low, but the consequence of prematurely stopping dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) in the first weeks after drug-eluting stent placement can be catastrophic. Therefore, the plan should be individualized rather than applying a uniform DAPT interruption strategy. The other options are less appropriate because stopping aspirin and continuing clopidogrel alone (option A) is not the usual evidence-based default. Stopping both agents for 7 days (option C) may expose the patient to unnecessary stent thrombosis risk. Low-molecular-weight heparin does not substitute for P2Y12 inhibition (option D).

questions.prompt: While imaging a right paratracheal node, multiple bright parallel lines at regular intervals suddenly appear on the EBUS monitor. Which next step is best?

options.label: Increase suction through the working channel.
Activate elastography before re-imaging.
Advance the needle to anchor the scope against the airway wall.
Adjust the saline-filled balloon against the airway wall.

questions.explanation: The bright parallel lines are classic reverberation artifact, and in CP-EBUS this commonly occurs when the ultrasound probe loses adequate contact with the airway wall. The corrective maneuver is to restore acoustic coupling by repositioning the scope with or without adjusting the saline-filled balloon so that it again contacts the mucosa smoothly. Once coupling is restored, the reverberation typically resolves and the node reappears clearly on gray-scale imaging. If balloon inflation is performed, care should be taken to inflate only just enough to achieve effective contact; overinflation can distort the airway wall, compress structures, or even damage the balloon, while underinflation promotes the artifact described in this stem. Option A is incorrect because suction does not fix an ultrasound coupling problem. Option B is incorrect because elastography depends on obtaining a usable base ultrasound image; it is not the first solution to reverberation from poor contact. Option C is incorrect and potentially unsafe because the needle should not be used as a stabilizing device against the airway wall.

questions.prompt: A patient with a left upper lobe primary lung cancer has a suspicious node seen along the left lateral tracheal wall, above the superior border of the aortic arch and below the thoracic inlet. Which IASLC nodal station is most consistent with this description?

options.label: 1L
4L
2L

questions.explanation: This node is station 2L (option D), the left upper paratracheal station. On the left side, the major landmark that separates upper from lower paratracheal region is the superior border of the aortic arch: nodes above that boundary are 2L, and nodes below it down to the upper rim of the left main pulmonary artery are 4L. Station 1L (option A) is a low cervical/supraclavicular station above the clavicles/manubrium and would imply N3 disease if positive. Station 4L (option B) lies below the superior border of the aortic arch. Station 5 (option C) is subaortic in the aortopulmonary window and lies lateral to the ligamentum arteriosum, not along the left lateral tracheal wall.

questions.prompt: Before puncturing a small, indistinct hilar target, an attempt is made to distinguish lymph node from possible adjacent vessels. An ultrasound mode is activated and a vessel is identified. The operator surmises that the flow within the vessel is away from the transducer. Which ultrasound mode was used?

options.label: M-mode
Power (energy) Doppler
Color Doppler
Elastography

questions.explanation: Color Doppler is the most appropriate choice when the operator needs to recognize blood flow and interpret its direction relative to the transducer. In standard convention, flow toward the transducer is usually displayed as red/orange and flow away from the transducer as blue. In CP-EBUS, this is especially useful immediately before needle puncture because it helps distinguish a vessel from a lymph node and decreases the chance of inadvertent vascular puncture. When the procedural question is, "Is this a vessel, and which way is blood moving?" color Doppler is the best answer. Option A is incorrect because M-mode displays motion over time and is not the routine mode used to map vascular direction before TBNA. Option B is incorrect because power (energy) Doppler reflects the intensity or amount of blood flow and is often more sensitive to low flow, but it does not provide directional information as directly as color Doppler. Option D is incorrect because elastography evaluates tissue stiffness, not vascular flow direction.

questions.prompt: A 63-year-old woman with a right lower lobe mass, multistation thoracic adenopathy and a pleural effusion undergoes EBUS-TBNA. After two passes of a large N3 station node, rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) shows abundant malignant cells consistent with likely adenocarcinoma. What is the best approach to further work up in this scenario?

options.label: Obtain at least two more passes for ROSE from the N3 lymph node and then conclude the procedure.
Obtain at least two more passes for cell block from the N3 lymph node and then conclude the procedure.
Conclude the procedure as a diagnosis has been established.
Complete EBUS-TBNA of the remaining nodal stations for both cell block and culture.

questions.explanation: Current thoracic pathology guidance emphasizes both adequate acquisition and careful specimen allocation. Once malignancy has been identified on site, the EBUS bronchoscopist should pivot to securing samples for not only definitive histologic diagnosis but also molecular marker testing. Modern lung-cancer care often depends on molecular profiling and other predictive biomarkers, especially for advanced disease as in this case, so specimen triage during the procedure is crucial. The best strategy is to continue sampling purposefully enough to support histologic classification and biomarker testing, while also avoiding a reflexively broad immunohistochemistry workup that needlessly consumes tissue. Current guidelines recommend at least 4 samples from a reliable target to ensure adequate tissue is obtained for both diagnosis and ancillary testing. Since the sampling of the lymph node in question provided ample tumor material on ROSE, at least two more passes secured in cell block for additional testing should suffice (option B correct). As ROSE already provided a diagnosis, no further samples for ROSE are required (option A incorrect). Since the diagnostic material was obtained from the highest stage node accessible by EBUS (N3), sampling of lower stage nodes is not necessary (option D incorrect). Concluding the procedure at this point (option C) may miss the opportunity to collect enough material for high-value downstream testing.

questions.prompt: A 64-year-old man undergoes EBUS-TBNA of a necrotic station 7 lymph node for lung cancer staging. Eight days later he presents with high fever, pleuritic chest pain, leukocytosis, and worsening retrosternal discomfort. 2-view CXR shows no new lung infiltrate or airspace disease, no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. However, there is subtle increased density and slight widening of the subcarinal space. What is the likely diagnosis?

options.label: Mediastinitis with abscess
Airway hemorrhage
Acute pulmonary embolism
Postprocedure inflammatory fever

questions.explanation: This presentation is consistent with delayed mediastinitis and mediastinal abscess formation after EBUS-TBNA. Infectious complications may declare themselves days to weeks after the procedure rather than immediately, and symptoms such as fever and chest pain should prompt urgent evaluation. Chest imaging should be performed if a diagnosis isn’t obvious by physical exam and history. Necrotic or cystic mediastinal targets appear to carry higher infectious risk because inoculated material may be poorly cleared. Management commonly requires broad-spectrum antibiotics, and some patients need surgical or image-guided drainage. The other options are less likely. Airway hemorrhage does not present 10 days post-procedure and would not typically be accompanied by fever and pleurisy. Acute pulmonary embolism (option C) does not explain the suggestion of a new mediastinal fluid collection, and a high fever is uncommon. Transient inflammatory fever (option D) is usually early, self-limited, and not associated with progressive chest symptoms.

questions.prompt: A novice EBUS bronchoscopist who is fairly skilled with routine therapeutic bronchoscopy has trouble intubating the trachea with the EBUS scope during a procedure without an airway adjunct. Once in the lower airway, they then repeatedly scrape the posterior airway wall and have difficulty with maneuvering, frequently getting ‘stuck’ at multiple carinae. What is the likely reason for this learning curve with EBUS bronchoscopy?

options.label: The EBUS scope is much larger in diameter than a conventional therapeutic bronchoscope
The endoscopic view of the EBUS scope is forward-oblique rather than a straight 0-degree view.
The working channel is too small to permit adequate suction of secretions.
The transducer scans 360 degrees around the axis of the bronchoscope.

questions.explanation: CP-EBUS does not behave like a standard straight-view flexible bronchoscope. The optical view is forward-oblique rather than purely straight ahead, and the tip is not completely visible during advancement. Because of this geometry, a novice operator must modify both insertion and scope maneuvering technique from previously acquired skill of routine white-light bronchoscopy. During intubation, the EBUS bronchoscopist should remain particularly careful while traversing the larynx with a gentle antero-(forward) flexion of the scope until just past the vocal cords, at which time a neutral position can be assumed. Similarly, during lower airway maneuvering, a neutral or slight forward flexion position of the scope helps avoid posterior wall trauma. Finally, because the scope tip is not clearly visible on endoscopic white-light view, the bronchoscopist should anticipate its position at airway bifurcations so to not overshoot the turn and get ‘stuck’ at the relevant carina. Understanding and becoming adept with forward-oblique optics is essential for safe and effective basic EBUS technique. Option A is incorrect because modern EBUS scopes are only slightly larger in outer diameter than a conventional therapeutic bronchoscope and would not account for the operating difficulties described in the stem. Option C is incorrect because CP-EBUS scopes have working channels adequate in size to permit suction of simple secretions. Option D is incorrect because 360-degree scanning describes radial-probe EBUS, not convex-probe EBUS.

questions.prompt: Immediately after needle puncture of a station 4R lymph node, the image below is seen on the EBUS monitor. What should be the immediate next step in technique?

options.label: Retract the needle back into the scope and reattempt puncture.
Retract the needle back into the scope and readjust the depth of the sheath-needle apparatus.
Stabilize the needle and ensure scope angulation and anchoring against the airway wall
Adjust the gain on the EBUS console.

questions.explanation: The image shown is of a reverberation artifact seen when contact between the airway wall and the probe/balloon is lost. This is a fairly common occurrence during needle puncture when the needle encounters some resistance to target penetration and results in the scope being pushed back/away from the airway wall, resulting in loss of contact. The resistance encountered can be at the level of the wall (e.g., cartilaginous ring) or the target itself (e.g., fibrotic/calcified node). The former scenario may prevent needle entry completely, while in the latter the needle has partially entered the target. The EBUS bronchoscopist should first troubleshoot which has occurred by stabilizing the needle and attempting to better anchor the scope against the airway wall (option C). A slight partial retraction of the needle to relieve resistance may also assist in re-apposition of wall and scope. Occasionally, if the scope-wall decoupling was significant, the bronchoscopist will visualize the incompletely penetrated needle on endoscopic view. Option A is not the first step as the needle may already be in the target. Complete retraction should only be attempted if troubleshooting fails to reacquire a target image to reliably guarantee sampling. Option B is incorrect because readjustment of the sheath would only be required if there was concern of sheath migration during needle puncture. Option D would not solve the issue of loss of airway-scope contact.

questions.prompt: A 71-year-old obese man with obstructive sleep apnea presents with multistation mediastinal adenopathy and a 1.8-cm right upper lobe lung nodule lacking a bronchus sign. He is booked for advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy. A fellow will assist/perform much of the case under supervision, and rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) is unavailable. Which anesthesia approach should be planned?

options.label: Moderate sedation with IV midazolam and fentanyl for the entire case
Deep sedation/general anesthesia (GA) using IV propofol and remifentanyl for the entire case
Moderate sedation for the convex-probe (cp)EBUS portion first, with escalation to deep/GA if needed for lung nodule sampling
Deep/GA for the nodule portion first, with de-escalation to moderate sedation if needed for cpEBUS nodal sampling

questions.explanation: This is a high-complexity, longer-duration, combined mediastinal-plus-peripheral procedure in a trainee setting. Furthermore, ROSE is not available which excludes the possibility of avoiding nodule sampling with an on-site confirmatory nodal diagnosis. Therefore, to ensure patient safety and tolerance as well as proper completion of all portions of the procedure, a deep sedation/general anesthesia approach for the entire case is the best plan (option B correct). Moderate sedation for the entire case may be reasonable for isolated EBUS when nodal sampling is the only goal, or in combined cpEBUS-peripheral cases when the primary lung lesion is large and easily accessible without the need of advanced peripheral techniques. ROSE is helpful when a nodal diagnosis is achieved, which obviates the need to sample the primary lesion. However, even with ROSE, the proceduralist must anticipate a possible on-site nondiagnostic result which would then mandate also targeting the lung nodule. Therefore, if sampling the peripheral lesion is a possibility, its ease of accessibility should guide the anesthesia and procedure plan. In this case the primary lesion will need to be sampled (no ROSE). Because it is small and in a challenging location, to maximize outcome it is best targeted using advanced guidance techniques under GA (option A incorrect). If ROSE were available, a phased approach to the procedure (e.g., either starting with moderate sedation and escalating to GA or vice versa) could be a reasonable strategy, but in this case ROSE is unavailable (choice C and D incorrect).

questions.prompt: A 66-year-old man with symptomatic gastroesophageal reflux and a suspicious left lower lobe lesion plus mediastinal adenopathy at stations 4R and 7 is scheduled for same-session cpEBUS-TBNA and advanced peripheral bronchoscopy. Which airway plan is most appropriate?

options.label: Supraglottic airway
CPAP with spontaneous breathing
Large endotracheal tube, ideally 8.5–9.0 mm if anatomy allows
No artificial airway; just nasal cannula oxygen

questions.explanation: Concomitant peripheral bronchoscopy and aspiration risk favor a general anesthesia (GA) approach using a large endotracheal tube (option C correct). The largest feasible endotracheal tube, usually 8.5–9.0 mm, should be used because it allows ventilation around the bronchoscope and better tolerates higher positive end-expiratory pressure and tidal volumes. A supraglottic airway is appropriate for most EBUS cases, especially with upper paratracheal sampling needs, but is less desirable when there is a reflux risk as in this case (option A incorrect). Option B may enhance reflux and be inadequate under GA. Option D does not provide the controlled airway and ventilatory needs for this case.

questions.prompt: A 70-year-old man has a right upper lobe mass, positron emission tomography (PET)–avid 4R and 7 nodes, and is scheduled for advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy with rapid on-site cytologic evaluation (ROSE) support. Which procedural plan is most appropriate?

options.label: Perform peripheral navigational bronchoscopy (PNB) only as the PET scan already shows mediastinal metastases.
Perform both cpEBUS and PNB under moderate sedation to maximize same-day discharge.
Sample the mediastinal nodes first with cpEBUS-TBNA, then proceed to PNB only if additional tissue is still needed
Sample the lung lesion first with PNB, then proceed to cpEBUS-TBNA only if additional tissue is needed.

questions.explanation: For an advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy supported by ROSE, mediastinal sampling with cpEBUS-TBNA should come first when there is a favorable chance to establish the diagnosis intraprocedurally, as in this case (option C correct). This is because cpEBUS nodal sampling has a lower risk and cost profile than PNB. Thus avoiding PNB if possible is desirable (choice D incorrect). While a positive PET scan in the setting of suspected lung cancer is suggestive of mediastinal metastases, the unacceptably high false positive rate (up to 25-40%) mandates confirmation with tissue sampling (choice A incorrect). Combined advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy is an outpatient procedure in a vast majority of cases, regardless of anesthesia approach (choice C incorrect).

questions.prompt: Which of the following sets contains only lymph node stations that are directly and routinely accessible by standard linear EBUS-TBNA from the airway?
questions.explanation: Standard linear EBUS-TBNA can routinely access stations including 2R, 2L, 4R, 4L, 7, 10R, 10L, 11R, and 11L. Therefore 4R, 7, and 10L are all accessible (option A). Stations 5 and 6 are not routinely sampled by standard airway EBUS, though in selected situations, station 5 can be approached via transvascular (left PA) approach. Stations 8 and 9 are typically accessed from the esophagus with EUS. Stations 3A (retrosternal) and 1R (lower cervical) are not typically accessible endoscopically.

questions.prompt: A 66-year-old man undergoes endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration (EBUS-TBNA) of a right lower paratracheal lymph node during lung cancer staging. Rapid on-site evaluation shows abundant small mature lymphocytes with scant cytoplasm, and the nuclei are fairly uniform and similar in size to nearby red blood cells. There are scattered bronchial epithelial cells with preserved polarity and cilia. Which cytologic interpretation is most accurate?

options.label: Granulomatous lymphadenitis
Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma
Nonrepresentative needle aspirate
Representative benign lymph node with respiratory contamination

questions.explanation: A representative benign lymph node aspirate obtained by EBUS-TBNA is usually dominated by small mature lymphocytes. These cells have a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, uniform chromatin, and nuclei that are often described as similar in size to adjacent red blood cells. Occasionally, during puncture the needle will pull in a few bronchial epithelial cells, ‘contaminating’ the lymph node aspirate. It is important to distinguish these bronchial cells from atypical squamous cells which could represent metastatic disease in the proper clinical setting. The bronchial epithelial cells described in the stem have normal (preserved polarity, presence of cilia) rather than atypical (polymorphism, no cilia) features. The combination of background normal lymph material and lack of atypical epithelial cells is most consistent with an aspirate representative of a lymph node without evidence of metastatic carcinoma (option D). Diagnosing granulomatous lymphadenitis (option A) would require presence of epithelioid histiocytes and granulomatous aggregates rather than a purely mature lymphoid population. Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (option B) would show atypical epithelial cells, often polygonal with more abundant cytoplasm and marked nuclear atypia compared to the lymphoid background. A nonrepresentative aspirate (option C) is suggested when the sample is mostly blood, bronchial contamination, or scant acellular material rather than true lymphoid tissue.

questions.prompt: A 64-year-old man presenting with dyspnea, weight loss, facial plethora and hyponatremia is found to have SVC syndrome and right mainstem bronchial narrowing related to bulky mediastinal adenopathy and a central lung mass. EBUS-TBNA shows dispersed and loosely cohesive sheets of malignant cells with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, and frequent mitotic figures. Immunohistochemistry is positive for synaptophysin and TTF-1, and the Ki-67 proliferation index is very high. Which diagnosis is most likely?

options.label: Lung adenocarcinoma
Small cell lung carcinoma
Squamous cell carcinoma
Typical carcinoid tumor

questions.explanation: Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC), also known as high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma (HGNEC), is an aggressive lung tumor classically presenting as a central lung mass often with bulky mediastinal adenopathy. As a consequence, it is frequently associated with mass effect of central thoracic structures, such as SVC syndrome and airway obstruction. SCLCs are also the lung tumor most commonly presenting with syndrome of inappropriate anti-diuretic hormone (SIADH)-related hyponatremia. Cytohistologically, SCLC is characterized by cells with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, finely granular chromatin, a high mitotic rate, and frequent crush artifact (more common with forceps biopsy than TBNA). A very high Ki-67 index is a defining feature of SCLC (HGNEC), and TTF-1 positivity is common. While adenocarcinomas also express TTF-1, they are more commonly a peripheral tumor, arising from clara cells or type 2 pneumocytes. Furthermore, they are not synaptophysin-positive (a neuroendocrine marker) and associated SIADH is extremely rare (option A incorrect). Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is also frequently a central airway tumor, arising from bronchial progenitor basal cells. However, they are easily distinguished morphologically from NECs by their characteristic cytohistology of large, polygonal cells with abundant cytoplasm and occasional keratinization and intercellular bridging (option C incorrect). Also, SCC is both synaptophysin and TTF-1 negative (but rather CK5/6 and p40/63 positive). Typical carcinoid tumor (option D) shows uniform cells in organized nests with few or no mitoses and a low/absent Ki67 level, an important distinguishing feature from SCLC.

questions.prompt: A 55-year-old man receives an EBUS bronchoscopy to evaluate a right upper lobe cavitary lesion and necrotic mediastinal adenopathy. ROSE of the first EBUS-TBNA pass from the 4R node shows granulomatous inflammation. Which specimen-handling plan is best for the tissue obtained from additional sampling?

options.label: Place remaining material in formalin for cytohistology testing
Place remaining material in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) solution and saline for flow cytometry, culture, and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing
Place remaining material on slides for further on-site cytology testing
Place remaining material in formalin and saline for cytohistology, culture, and PCR testing.

questions.explanation: The clinical presentation suggests either a primary lung cancer or cavitary pneumonia with adenopathy. Focal granulomatous thoracic disease is a classic lung-cancer impersonator, with mycobacterial and endemic fungal infections being primary considerations. Because ROSE demonstrated granuloma, an underlying malignancy is less likely and infection more likely. Regardless, diagnosis of infection requires a synergistic approach, maximized by a combination of cytohistologic and microbiological analysis. Therefore, in this scenario the bronchoscopist should triage the sampled material accordingly. Proper processing material for these tests include formalin and saline (option D correct). Placing material in formalin only (option A) preserves morphology but sacrifices culture testing as organisms do not grow in formalin. RPMI is a lymphocyte preservative for flow cytometry testing used to assess for lymphoma, which is not a major diagnostic consideration in this case (option B incorrect). ROSE has provided a suspected diagnosis, and sampling focus should shift toward obtaining material for definitive testing (option C incorrect).

questions.prompt: A 64-year-old man with a 45-pack-year smoking history undergoes PET-CT for a 2.6-cm right upper lobe adenocarcinoma. EBUS-TBNA is malignant at station 4R and negative at stations 7 and 11R. There is no evidence of other metastases. Given the available information, which of the following is the correct TNM-overall stage pairing according to the 9th edition of the IASLC/UICC TNM lung cancer staging classification?

options.label: T1aN1M0--Stage IIA
T1cN2aM0--Stage IIB
T1cN2M0—Stage IIIA
T1cN3Mo--Stage IIIB

questions.explanation: A 2.6-cm primary is T1c. Involvement of a single ipsilateral mediastinal station is N2a. Absence of distant disease is M0. The correct grouping is therefore cT1cN2aM0, which is stage IIB in the TNM ninth edition (option B correct). Option A is incorrect because the nodule is larger than 10mm (not T1a) and a mediastinal, not hilar node is involved (not N1). Option C represents the N stage (2) and overall stage (IIIA) according to the 8th, not 9th TNM edition. Option D is incorrect because an ipsilateral, not contralateral (N3) node is involved.

questions.prompt: A 58-year-old woman with recurrent lung cancer is undergoing cpEBUS-TBNA for targeted sampling of a single enlarged station 2R lymph node. Because of narcotic tolerance due to chronic pain, a previous case initiated with moderate sedation was prematurely aborted due to patient agitation, and thus general anesthesia is planned for the current procedure. Otherwise, the patient has no significant reflux or aspiration risk, and peripheral bronchoscopy is not planned. Which airway conduit is most appropriate?

options.label: Supraglottic airway
A 7.5-mm endotracheal tube (ETT)
Rigid bronchoscope
High-flow nasal oxygen

questions.explanation: For EBUS-TBNA under general anesthesia, current guidance suggests a supraglottic airway (option A correct). This approach is especially important for upper tracheal evaluation and station 2 sampling, as in this case. A 7.5mm ETT would crowd the EBUS scope, which typically have an outer diameter larger than 6mm. This would promote hypoventilation and air trapping during the case. Furthermore, an ETT is not ideal for upper paratracheal sampling (option B incorrect). Rigid bronchoscopy is unnecessary for routine nodal sampling (option C incorrect). High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) oxygen would be the good approach if this patient was to have the procedure under moderate sedation. However, HFNC does not provide ventilatory support during general anesthesia (option D incorrect).

questions.prompt: A 71-year-old woman has a 3.8-cm left lower lobe adenocarcinoma and multistation adenopathy. She receives combined EBUS/EUS-B-TBNA during which nodal stations 4L, 7 and 8L are found to be involved with malignancy. Which nodal category is most appropriate?

options.label: N1
N2a
N2b
N3

questions.explanation: Stations 4L, 7 and 8L are all considered mediastinal nodes ipsilateral to the primary tumor. According to the 9th edition of the TNM staging scheme for lung cancer, N2 nodal stage is further subclassified as single- (N2a) and multi-node involvement (N2b), given the worse prognosis of the latter. As this is multistation N2 disease, N2b is the correct stage (option C). Option A is incorrect because N1 is limited to ipsilateral pulmonary (12-14), interlobar (11) or hilar (10) nodes. Option B is incorrect because N2a requires only one ipsilateral mediastinal or the subcarinal station. Option C is incorrect because N3 implies metastases to contralateral mediastinal or hilar nodes, or supraclavicular or scalene involvement, not seen in this case. With a 3.8-cm tumor, the full designation would be cT2aN2bM0, which is classified as an overall stage IIIB in the TNM ninth edition.

questions.prompt: A 64-year-old woman with heavy smoking history has a 4.2 cm spiculated peripheral right upper lobe lung mass. PET/CT shows multiple mediastinal nodes that are all subcentimeter in size and without significant FDG avidity. She is an operative candidate. Which is the best next step in management?

options.label: Proceed to right upper lobectomy
CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy of the lung mass
Mediastinoscopy for directed mediastinal staging of the right paratracheal space
Systematic cpEBUS-TBNA mediastinal staging

questions.explanation: A large lung cancer (>30mm in size), as is highly suspected in this case, carries a higher risk of occult mediastinal metastasis, regardless of the mass’ location (peripheral vs. central) or apparent benignity of the nodes on thoracic imaging (<10mm in size and negative PET). Therefore, invasive mediastinal staging is recommended, with endosonography favored as the initial approach when the relevant stations are accessible (option D correct). Systematic cpEBUS allows minimally invasive sampling of the key paratracheal, subcarinal, and hilar/interlobar nodal stations that could alter management. Proceeding directly to lobectomy (option A incorrect) would underestimate the false-negative rate of imaging for occult mediastinal disease in large tumors. “PET-negative” should not be considered “adequately staged” when pretest nodal metastatic risk is elevated because of central tumor location, large size (>30mm), or N1 node-positivity. While a negative PET/CT may add some reassurance clinically, its limited sensitivity to detect occult micrometastases would not reliably obviate the need for nodal sampling before surgical resection. Option C is incorrect because mediastinoscopy is reserved for selected cases when suspicion for nodal metastases persists after an initial negative EBUS-TBNA (e.g., enlarged or fluorodeoxyglucose-avid node). Transthoracic biopsy of the mass is unnecessary as the lesion is already highly suspicious for a primary lung cancer and the procedure would not stage the mediastinum, thus delaying the pre-operative work up (option B incorrect).

questions.prompt: Tissue sampling is planned in a patient with suspected advanced lung cancer. The primary lesion is in the right upper lobe and 2.8-cm in size. There is multifocal adenopathy, including in the 4R, 7 and 4L stations. Rapid on-site evaluation is available. Which approach is best?

options.label: Advanced peripheral navigational bronchoscopy (PNB) first, then cpEBUS only if ROSE is negative.
CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy (CT-TTNB) of the right upper lobe mass, then cpEBUS if CT-TTNB is nondiagnostic
cpEBUS first, then PNB only if ROSE is negative.
Both cpEBUS and PNB in either sequence, regardless of ROSE results

questions.explanation: In this patient with suspected lung cancer and mediastinal node involvement, planning for combined advanced diagnostic bronchoscopy (cpEBUS+PNB) is most appropriate. The presence of ROSE allows for on-site assessment. Targeting the lymph nodes first may solidify a preliminary diagnosis and simultaneously stage the mediastinum, thus obviating the need for lung sampling and limiting procedure-related risk. Therefore, the most efficient sequence within this context of suspected mediastinal disease and ROSE availability is to start with cpEBUS (option C correct). While in modern practice PNB is also considered very safe (< 3% pneumothorax risk, < 5% overall risk for severe complication), a PNB-first strategy will prolong the procedure in a proportion of patients, as cpEBUS staging must still be performed (option A incorrect). CT-guided transthoracic biopsy does not stage the mediastinum and also carries a higher risk of pneumothorax compared to modern advanced bronchoscopy, with minimal, if any advantage of diagnostic yield (option B incorrect). The primary benefit of ROSE in this setting is to limit unnecessary sampling and procedure duration if a nodal diagnosis and stage are established by cpEBUS intraoperatively. Therefore, the sequence matters if the chance of nodal disease is high (option D incorrect).

questions.prompt: During a complete CP-EBUS mediastinal staging examination for a right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer, rapid on-site evaluation from station 7 shows metastatic carcinoma. Station 4R is also enlarged. What is the best next step?

options.label: Complete sample acquisition from station 7 to ensure tissue for molecular marker analysis and conclude the procedure.
Conclude the procedure and refer for mediastinoscopy for 4R sampling.
Complete sample acquisition from station 7 to ensure tissue for molecular marker analysis and then continue to station 4R for further cpEBUS sampling.
Conclude cpEBUS and switch to peripheral navigation bronchoscopy and sample the primary lesion.

questions.explanation: The best next step is to continue systematic sampling of the remaining relevant mediastinal stations. Recent AABIP guidance highlights the practical impact of the ninth edition TNM classification, which subdivides N2 disease into single-station (N2a) and multistation (N2b) involvement. Because that distinction influences prognosis and treatment discussions, a positive ROSE result from one N2 mediastinal station should not automatically end a systematic staging examination if additional relevant N2 stations remain accessible (option C correct). Stopping after the first positive N2 node (option A incorrect) reflects a reasonable approach consistent with the previous, TNM 8th edition. Mediastinoscopy (option B incorrect) is not required as CP-EBUS will adequately sample the 4R node. Sampling the primary lesion (option D) may help downstream molecular analysis, but it does not complete mediastinal staging.

questions.prompt: A 70-year-old man with suspected metastatic non-small cell lung cancer has a 3.6-cm left upper lobe mass and enlarged fluorodeoxyglucose-avid 4L and 7 nodes. The oncologist emphasizes the need for a high probability of successful broad molecular profiling from the initial procedure. Which single sampling strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: Perform advanced peripheral navigational bronchoscopy with cryobiopsy of the primary lung cancer
Perform cpEBUS-TBNA nodal sampling with rapid on-site assessment
CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy (TTNB)
Surgical wedge resection of the primary lung cancer

questions.explanation: All of the choices listed can provide adequate tissue for simultaneous diagnosis and molecular characterization of lung cancer. However, cpEBUS-guided nodal sampling offers a high yield with low risk and efficient resource utilization (option B correct). Option A will require resources of general anesthesia and cryobiopsy to achieve what cpEBUS can achieve with needle sampling under moderate sedation. CT-TTNB has a much higher pneumothorax risk compared to cpEBUS and a similar yield (option C incorrect). Surgical resection is unnecessary given the less invasive options available and would not be pursued for advanced disease (option D incorrect).

questions.prompt: Which option most appropriately reflects the core actionable genomic targeting approach obtained from cpEBUS-TBNA samples?

options.label: EGFR and ALK only
PD-L1 and thyroid transcription factor 1 only
EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, and NTRK, with broader panel coverage often also detecting other clinically useful alterations
KRAS only, because it is the most common driver

questions.explanation: : The logic behind comprehensive profiling on cpEBUS material is that advanced NSCLC may harbor several distinct actionable alterations, including point mutations, exon-skipping events, and gene fusions. A broad panel is preferred because it can evaluate multiple relevant biomarkers from the same small specimen rather than forcing the laboratory to spend tissue on separate sequential tests. This is especially important when the mediastinal aspirate is the only diagnostic material available. Option A is incorrect because it is too narrow for contemporary practice. Option B is incorrect because PD-L1 and lineage markers do not replace genomic profiling. Option D is incorrect because focusing on a single common driver would miss several less common but highly actionable targets.

questions.prompt: A community teaching hospital has just obtained a mediastinal cryobiopsy system. No bronchoscopist there has previously performed cpEBUS-guided cryobiopsy. Based on current evidence, how should this institution develop their cpEBUS-nodal cryobiopsy program?

options.label: Make cryobiopsy the default for all malignant nodes given its utility for molecular analysis
Restrict cryobiopsy to nodes larger than 2 cm
Use cryobiopsy when infection is suspected
Restrict cryobiopsy to cases with negative ROSE or prior nondiagnostic TBNA.

questions.explanation: Mediastinal cryobiopsy should be selectively and carefully introduced into a new program. Contemporary guidance suggests that it should be performed by experienced operators and is best used in selected scenarios such as negative ROSE, prior nondiagnostic EBUS-TBNA, or when there is strong suspicion for lymphoma, granulomatous disease, or uncommon pathology where larger tissue fragments are likely to add value (option D correct). Option A is incorrect because cryobiopsy is not clearly superior to standard cpEBUS-TBNA for molecular anlaysis, given the latter techniques high performance in this setting. Option B is incorrect because node size alone is not the indication for making cryobiopsy first-line. Option C is incorrect because TBNA provides adequate material for culture processing. A thoughtful cryobiopsy implementation plan should start with patient selection, operator training, rescue-use cases, and institutional readiness rather than wholesale replacement of standard cpEBUS-TBNA.

questions.prompt: A patient with suspected metastatic left lung adenocarcinoma has three accessible nodes on cpEBUS: an 8mm, round and well-circumscribed 11L node, a 19mm, partially necrotic station 7 node, and a 13mm intensely FDG-avid 4R node. Which is the best choice to maximize chances of successful comprehensive next-generation sequencing (NGS) tumor genetic analysis?

options.label: The 11R node
The 7 node
The 4R node
Any node, as long as malignant cells are identified on rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE)

questions.explanation: Successful NGS depends more on the quality of tumor recovery than on a single anatomic feature such as size. Studies of EBUS-based profiling show that higher tumor cellularity, more representative core tissue, and more advanced nodal disease can all favor successful comprehensive testing. In practice, the best target is the safest accessible node that appears most likely to yield abundant viable tumor, not merely the largest node encountered. In this scenario, the FDG-avid mildly enlarged 4R node, representing a N3 lesion, is most likely to yield the most quality material for NGS success (option C correct). Option A is incorrect because smaller size does not confer molecular superiority. Option B is incorrect because size alone is an unreliable proxy for nucleic acid quality, especially if necrosis is present, which predicts a poorly viable sample. Option D is incorrect because malignant confirmation alone does not mean all nodes are equally suitable for comprehensive profiling.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the current role of biomarkers in the assessment of suspected lung cancer and ability to reduce both unnecessary procedures and delays in diagnosis?

options.label: Models that combine clinical risk, radiomics, and blood-based tests are better than relying on a single assay
Serum protein markers provide accurate mediastinal staging in lieu of tissue sampling
Plasma next-generation genomic sequencing has replaced tissue diagnosis
Biomarkers are most useful for postoperative recurrence surveillance

questions.explanation: No single “magic bullet” biomarker is likely to dominate the diagnostic and staging landscape of lung cancer. Different biomarker classes capture different aspects of tumor biology or host response: proteins, autoantibodies, methylation, fragmentomics, microRNAs, breath signatures, airway transcriptomics, and radiomics each contribute partial information. Option A is therefore the best answer because multimodal integration is the most plausible path to improving both specificity and sensitivity in real-world practice. This is especially relevant for procedural vs. surveillance triage. Option B is incorrect because protein markers are unlikely to effectively stage a tumor with the granularity currently used with the TNM classification, even after effective diagnosis. Option C is incorrect because plasma sequencing cannot replace tissue diagnosis in all patients, especially early-stage or low-shedding lesions. Option D is incorrect because the greatest unmet need in this context is upfront nodule triage rather than surveillance after definitive treatment.

questions.prompt: A 70-year-old former smoker has a 4.3-cm spiculated right upper lobe mass and a second 1.8-cm left lower lobe nodule. Both are FDG-avid. There are several non-enlarged, PET-negative lymph nodes in the mediastinum. What is the best tissue evaluation strategy for this patient?

options.label: Same-session advanced peripheral navigational bronchoscopic (PNB) sampling of both lung lesions, with cpEBUS mediastinal staging.
Sample the dominant mass and mediastinal nodes by combined PNB+cpEBUS, perform CT-guided biopsy of the smaller nodule in a separate session.
Perform PNB of the larger mass and cpEBUS mediastinal staging, observe the smaller nodule.
Proceed to resection of the dominant mass, observe the smaller nodule.

questions.explanation: In patients with multiple synchronous pulmonary nodules, same-session bronchoscopic sampling can be highly efficient in providing useful information for treatment planning. Histologically distinguishing among the potential combinations of diagnostic possibilities will have important implications on management. This patient has two, bilateral lung lesions. Also, while the lymph nodes do not overtly suggest pathology, because the right lung mass is large and highly suspicious for lung cancer, occult mediastinal involvement is possible and warrants tissue sampling rather than relying on imaging alone. The smaller left lung lesion can represent a metastatic focus, which would upstage the patient to M1a (Stage IVA). It may also represent a second, early-stage lung cancer or a non-malignant diagnosis. Therefore, because modern advanced PNB allows for comprehensive, efficient, and safe same-session multilesion sampling, it is the preferred choice in such a scenario (option A correct). Option B is incorrect because it unnecessarily prolongs the diagnostic pathway and adds procedural risk when a more efficient option is available. Option C may leave the most clinically important distinction unresolved (two separate diagnoses vs. a stage IVA tumor) and does not leverage the efficiency advantage of the same-session combined bronchoscopic strategy. Option D risks overtreatment or incorrect stage assignment without adequate tissue characterization.

questions.prompt: A 52-year-old woman has cough, anterior uveitis, and bihilar thoracic adenopathy. She denies dyspnea, fever, chills, weight loss or night sweats. CT confirms the thoracic findings, with bulky nodes at stations 11R and 11L, but no parenchymal lung disease. Which is the appropriate next step in evaluation?

options.label: Video-assisted thoracoscopic lung biopsy (VATS)
cpEBUS-guided lymph node sampling
Mediastinoscopy
Computed tomography-guided core lung biopsy

questions.explanation: This patient has suspected sarcoidosis, with another autoimmune phenomenon or atypical infection also possible. Absent another easily targeted lesion such as on the skin, contemporary guidance recommends cpEBUS-guided lymph node sampling as the initial invasive procedure (option B correct). Option C is incorrect because mediastinoscopy is more invasive and is generally reserved for selected cases after less invasive sampling is nondiagnostic or unavailable. In addition, in this case only hilar nodal stations appear involved, which are not within reach of mediastinoscopy. Option A and D are incorrect because lung biopsy is less yielding when there is no apparent parenchymal disease; also, VATS is the most invasive option listed and would not be an initial choice.

questions.prompt: A 66-year-old man with a right upper lobe squamous cell carcinoma is undergoing systematic convex-probe endobronchial ultrasound (cpEBUS) staging. From the distal trachea, the operator identifies a hypoechoic node to the right of the trachea (FIGURE). On computed tomography, the node lies below the caudal margin of the left brachiocephalic (innominate) vein and above the lower rim of the azygos vein. Which International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer (IASLC) nodal station is being sampled?

options.label: 2R
4R

questions.explanation: This is station 4R (option B). The right lower paratracheal station extends from the intersection of the caudal margin of the left brachiocephalic (innominate) vein with the trachea down to the inferior border of the azygos vein. The SVC runs longitudinally in this area and the azygous can be visualized emptying into it. For cpEBUS operators, recognizing this vertical window is essential because 4R is one of the highest-yield and most commonly sampled mediastinal stations during lung cancer staging. It also carries direct staging implications: if malignant in a patient with a right lung primary, 4R represents ipsilateral mediastinal disease (N2), not hilar disease (N1). Therefore, distinguishing it from a node in the 10R station (option C), which extends from the inferior border of the azygous along the lateral wall of the right mainstem bronchus until its bifurcation, is critical. Station 2R (option A) lies above the caudal margin of the brachiocephalic vein as it crosses the trachea. Station 7 (option D) is subcarinal and lies below the carina between the main bronchi.

questions.prompt: During CP-EBUS the image of a subcarinal node becomes diffusely overbright, and contrast between the node and surrounding tissue is poor even though balloon contact is good and Doppler is turned off. Which adjustment is most likely to restore useful gray-scale contrast?

options.label: Increase needle excursion.
Inflate the balloon further until the airway wall indents.
Switch immediately to ROSE.
Reduce the overall gain on the ultrasound processor.

questions.explanation: Gain controls the amplification of returning echo signals and therefore the overall brightness of the ultrasound image. When gain is set too high, the image becomes washed out and contrast between the target node and surrounding tissue falls, even if contact and probe position are otherwise good. Lowering the gain is the most direct way to restore useful gray-scale discrimination in this scenario. Option A is incorrect because needle excursion changes puncture depth, not image brightness. Option B is incorrect because overinflating the balloon can distort the airway wall and is not the appropriate solution when contact is already adequate. Option C is incorrect because ROSE is a cytology workflow that occurs after sampling and does not troubleshoot a poor ultrasound image. In practical knobology, fellows should think first of gain when the picture is globally too bright, just as they should think of coupling when they see reverberation from poor contact.

questions.prompt: A 61-year-old man with a heavy smoking history has a 3.9 cm left upper lobe spiculated central lung mass, multistation enlarged mediastinal nodes, and a 2.7 cm fluorodeoxyglucose-avid liver lesion. Assuming the patient is a reasonable medical candidate for all of the listed options, what is the appropriate approach in evaluating this patient’s suspected lung cancer?

options.label: Percutaneous biopsy of the liver lesion
CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy of the lung mass
Mediastinoscopy of the largest mediastinal node
Systematic cpEBUS-TBNA of mediastinal lymph nodes

questions.explanation: For a patient undergoing evaluation for likely advanced lung cancer, if a safely accessible and highly suspicious distant metastatic site is present, first sampling that site is an efficient strategy since it can establish both diagnosis and stage IV disease in a single procedure (option A correct). If the pathology is consistent with metastatic lung cancer, invasive mediastinal staging may not be needed to decide the initial treatment pathway. Sampling the primary lung mass first (option B incorrect) may establish malignancy but does not stage the patient. Mediastinoscopy could establish a diagnosis but provides only partial staging and also is not the recommended first strategy for mediastinal assessment (option C incorrect). cpEBUS is the recommended first method for establishing suspected mediastinal disease in this setting. However, the question of distant disease would remain unresolved (option D incorrect). An important caveat to this scenario is that the suspected extrathoracic metastatic site must be technically accessible, clinically appropriate to sample, and also likely to yield abundant enough material for comprehensive tumor characterization (diagnosis + molecular analysis). A multidisciplinary discussion may be beneficial in these selected cases.

questions.prompt: A patient with a right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer has sonographically visible station 4L, station 7, and station 11R nodes at the time of staging cpEBUS. Which planned sampling sequence will minimize the risk of false upstaging from needle contamination?
