questions.explanation: Systematic nodal sampling should proceed from the highest to lowest potential stage to reduce the risk that malignant cells carried on the needle contaminate a lower-stage station and create a false impression of more advanced disease. For a right upper lobe primary cancer, station 4L is an N3 node, station 7 is N2, and station 11R is N1; therefore 4L -> 7 -> 11R is the correct sequence (option C correct). This N3-to-N2-to-N1 logic is a core staging principle when planning the approach to cpEBUS mediastinal staging. The other sequences all start with lower-stage ipsilateral nodes and move upward (options A, B, D incorrect).

questions.prompt: A patient is being evaluated for the first time for suspected sarcoidosis and cpEBUS-TBNA of thoracic nodes is planned. Which of the following is an appropriate technical approach to the procedure?

options.label: A 21-gauge needle is preferred
If rapid on-site cytologic evaluation is unavailable, 3 needle passes from a single node are sufficient for diagnosis
At least 20 agitations per needle pass is needed for suspected granulomatous disease
Intranodal cryobiopsy should be the default sampling technique

questions.explanation: Current EBUS guidance supports the use of either 21-gauge or 22-gauge needles for routine EBUS-TBNA. A later meta-analysis suggested that 19-gauge needles may have higher sensitivity in sarcoidosis, but the evidence base was limited and largely observational. Furthermore, operators have reported more technical difficulty with 19G puncture. This matters in angulated stations such as 4L, where maneuverability can influence real-world performance as much as theoretical tissue volume. Consequently, 19G use has not translated into a firm recommendation to routinely replace standard 21/22-gauge practice. Option B is incorrect because a prospective multicenter study in stage I/II sarcoidosis found that when ROSE is unavailable at least 4 passes per patient provide a pragmatic target, whether obtained from a single lesion or distributed across two lesions. Option C is incorrect because randomized data found no meaningful improvement in diagnostic yield or specimen adequacy when the number of intranodal revolutions was increased from 10 to 20. Therefore, at least 10 agitations per pass is recommended as a reasonable default technical target for most situations. Finally, current EBUS guidance allows EBUS bronchoscopists to consider intranodal forceps biopsy or mediastinal cryobiopsy after negative ROSE or prior nondiagnostic EBUS-TBNA. Randomized and meta-analytic data suggest that these techniques can improve yield in benign mediastinal disease, including sarcoidosis. However, these approaches require expertise and may increase cost, and thus do not replace routine 21/22G needle technique as the default standard for sampling suspected granulomatous adenopathy (option D incorrect).

questions.prompt: A 44-year-old man with fever, weight loss, and multistation mediastinal and hilar adenopathy undergoes CP-EBUS. Which sampling plan is most guideline-concordant?

options.label: Four passes combined across nodes, utilizing direct smears for sample processing.
Two passes per relevant node, utilizing formalin-fixed cell block and microbiology triage for processing.
Three passes per relevant node, utilizing formalin-fixed cell block only.
Four or more passes per relevant node, with material triaged for cell block plus saline and/or Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) medium as clinically indicated.

questions.explanation: When non-carcinoma diagnoses such as granulomatous disease or lymphoma are being considered, CP-EBUS sampling generally needs to be broader than a routine carcinoma-only workflow. Current guidance supports obtaining 4 or more passes per relevant node and triaging material for the likely downstream tests: cell block for histopathology, nonbacteriostatic saline for microbiology or molecular microbiologic testing, and RPMI medium for flow cytometry when lymphoma is in the differential. A minimalist smear-only approach (option A) or a two-pass strategy (option B) risks inadequate diagnostic material. A formalin-fixed cell block alone (option C) would also be incomplete in a case where infection and lymphoma are both realistic possibilities. Advanced tools such as intranodal forceps or cryobiopsy may be considered in selected difficult cases, but they do not replace correct specimen triage.

questions.prompt: A 66-year-old man with a newly diagnosed 3.8 cm peripheral right upper lobe lung mass which is highly suspicious for lung cancer is being considered for lobectomy. Computed tomography (CT) and positron emission tomography (PET) show no hilar or mediastinal adenopathy or uptake. He is medically operable and has no evidence of distant metastasis. Which next step is most guideline-concordant?

options.label: Proceed to lobectomy
Perform systematic convex-probe endobronchial ultrasound (CP-EBUS)-guided mediastinal staging
Sample the primary lesion by CT-guided needle biopsy
Blood biomarker assessment

questions.explanation: A peripheral non-small cell lung cancer larger than 3 cm carries a meaningful risk of occult mediastinal metastasis even when CT and PET are negative. Recent AABIP guidance advises to recognize tumor size greater than 3 cm as a significant risk factor and to consider invasive mediastinal staging in otherwise imaging node-negative patients. When CP-EBUS is chosen for staging, current guidance favors a systematic examination rather than a targeted biopsy of only obviously abnormal nodes, because occult disease can be found in normal-sized or PET-negative stations. Proceeding directly to surgery (option A) may miss N2 disease and alter treatment selection. Sampling only the primary tumor (option C) may confirm the diagnosis but leaves the mediastinum unstaged. Also, modern guidance suggests advanced peripheral bronchoscopy as the preferred choice for primary lesion sampling due to its favorable safety profile and versatility. Biomarkers do not substitute for a tissue diagnosis (option D incorrect)
